name: royal blue eveningstar mace
shortDescription: A polished eveningstar mace finished in royal blue, built for captains who want authority to show before they ever raise a hand. The painted haft and bright head make it sell well to guards, duelists, and collectors of ceremonial force.
flavorText: “Blue paint does not soften iron, but it does make the threat look appointed.”

name: skullcrusher
shortDescription: A heavy mace with a brutal nickname and the sort of balance that tells buyers it was made for decisive work. Arena brokers and border guards ask for it by name, though careful merchants keep it wrapped until serious customers arrive.
flavorText: “Some weapons need decoration. This one brought a reputation instead.”

name: small long mace
shortDescription: A narrow mace with extra reach, light enough for quick hands but long enough to keep trouble at a careful distance. It is favored by caravan escorts who need a sidearm that fits beside packs and saddles.
flavorText: “Long enough to matter, small enough to travel.”

name: small long royal blue mace
shortDescription: A slim travel mace painted in royal blue, combining parade color with practical reach. It is popular with junior officers, noble messengers, and anyone who wants a weapon that looks assigned rather than improvised.
flavorText: “The color says service. The iron says service may become unpleasant.”

name: small mace
shortDescription: A compact iron mace that can be tucked behind a counter, packed in a wagon, or carried through a crowded market without much comment. Plain, reliable, and easy to sell to cautious folk.
flavorText: “Not every problem on the road is large enough for a grand weapon.”

name: small morningstar
shortDescription: A short morningstar with a spiked head and a grip worn smooth from practice swings. It has the unpleasant honesty of a tool made for close, messy moments, which keeps its price steady among guards.
flavorText: “Small does not mean gentle when spikes are involved.”

name: small royal blue mace
shortDescription: A compact mace dressed in royal blue paint, often bought for palace guards, courier escorts, and young nobles learning to look dangerous. Beneath the color is a simple, dependable lump of metal.
flavorText: “Court color on market iron, and somehow both are true.”

name: small royal blue morningstar
shortDescription: A short morningstar with a royal blue haft and a sharply businesslike head. It catches the eye on display racks, which is useful because most buyers decide whether they trust a weapon before touching it.
flavorText: “A bright handle makes the spikes look almost polite. Almost.”

name: undead hunter
shortDescription: A specialized weapon with pale bindings, old blessings, and a name that makes village elders pay attention. Whether every charm still works is debatable, but gravewardens and monster hunters value the confidence it carries.
flavorText: “Half iron, half promise, and sold mostly to people who need both.”

name: ankus
shortDescription: A hooked ankus used by beast handlers, temple processions, and caravan crews managing large animals. Its shape is practical rather than cruel, meant to guide weight and momentum with a small turn of the wrist.
flavorText: “The smallest hook in the yard can steer the largest temper.”

name: strong spear
shortDescription: A sturdy spear with a thick shaft and a well-seated head, made for buyers who care more about surviving work than admiring polish. It is a dependable staple for militias, hunters, and roadside guards.
flavorText: “A spear is simple until it saves the whole line.”

name: scarce crystal spear
shortDescription: A rare spear set with a glassy crystal head that catches light in sharp, unsettling flashes. Collectors prize its impossible elegance, while practical fighters debate whether beauty this fragile-looking can truly be trusted.
flavorText: “It points like ice and prices like a secret.”

name: fabled trident of Sundering
shortDescription: A fabled trident with three severe prongs and a name attached to old harbor songs. Sailors claim it parts stormwater and stubborn locks alike, which is enough to keep bidding lively whenever one appears.
flavorText: “Some tools divide fish. This one divides stories from silence.”

name: notable starcourt spear
shortDescription: A notable spear decorated with star-court markings, likely made for a ceremonial guard or a very proud champion. The metal is sound, but its real value lies in the noble crestwork and courtly rumor.
flavorText: “Stars on the shaft, debts in the ledger.”

name: fancy lajatang
shortDescription: A fancy lajatang with blades at both ends and careful ornament along the grip. It demands skill, room, and a confident buyer, which narrows the market but raises the price among trained duelists.
flavorText: “A weapon for those who believe one blade is merely a suggestion.”

name: long glaive
shortDescription: A long glaive with a broad cutting blade mounted on a reach-ready pole. It is awkward indoors, excellent at a gate, and much loved by guards who prefer trouble to arrive no closer than necessary.
flavorText: “Distance is a kind of armor, if the haft is long enough.”

name: ironclan glaive
shortDescription: A clan-styled glaive with heavy iron fittings and marks from a forge tradition that values endurance over delicacy. It carries a stern look that sells well among border houses and veteran escorts.
flavorText: “Clan iron rarely flatters the hand, but it remembers its work.”

name: royal spear
shortDescription: A polished spear with formal trim, suitable for palace ranks, banners, and anyone who needs a weapon to look organized. The head is practical, but the finish speaks of orders and uniforms.
flavorText: “A straight line of royal spears can make even a nervous street behave.”

name: rough long bardiche
shortDescription: A long bardiche with a rough haft, broad blade, and few concessions to comfort. It feels like a frontier answer to heavy problems, the sort of polearm bought with winter patrols in mind.
flavorText: “No polish, no apology, plenty of edge.”

name: simple halberd
shortDescription: A plain halberd combining hook, point, and chopping blade without decorative fuss. Its versatility makes it a sensible purchase for town guards who have to handle gates, horses, and bad decisions.
flavorText: “Simple, because the work is complicated enough.”

name: simple trident
shortDescription: A straightforward trident with three clean prongs and a sturdy shaft. Fisherfolk know its practical use, guards know its reach, and coastal buyers appreciate a weapon that does not pretend to be inland.
flavorText: “Three points, one price, many uses.”

name: small glaive
shortDescription: A shorter glaive made for tighter roads, narrow bridges, and merchants who cannot spare wagon space for a full polearm. It loses some reach but keeps the useful bite of a curved blade.
flavorText: “A glaive trimmed down for alleys and impatient caravans.”

name: small lajatang
shortDescription: A compact lajatang with twin blades and a grip built for close, practiced motion. It is less theatrical than full-length versions, but far easier to transport and display in a crowded stall.
flavorText: “Two blades, less room, same demand for skill.”

name: rough spear
shortDescription: A rough spear with a serviceable point and a shaft that still shows the maker’s haste. It is not pretty, but it is cheap, sturdy enough, and useful wherever danger costs more than lumber.
flavorText: “The road seldom asks whether the spear was sanded nicely.”

name: fabled spear of Pearlgate
shortDescription: A fabled spear associated with Pearlgate tales, sea mist, and moonlit harbor defenses. Its pale inlays make it especially desirable to coastal nobles who collect proof of old maritime glory.
flavorText: “Pearlgate closed once. This spear keeps explaining why.”

name: expensive spear
shortDescription: A costly spear with refined fittings, straight grain, and a head polished to a disciplined shine. It is bought by those who could afford plainer protection but prefer their preparedness to be admired.
flavorText: “A spear can be practical and still know its own price.”

name: ceremonial spear with gems
shortDescription: A ceremonial spear set with small gems and careful metal bands, built more for procession than patrol. Still, the point is real enough to discourage anyone tempted to treat ceremony as weakness.
flavorText: “The gems catch the crowd. The point catches mistakes.”

name: notable short spear of reaching
shortDescription: A notable short spear whose charm-work supposedly extends its threat beyond the expected measure. Buyers test it with careful fingers and long looks, because even rumors of extra reach change a fight.
flavorText: “Short in the rack, longer in the stories.”

name: strong splitting spear
shortDescription: A strong spear with a widened, splitting head made to bite hard into shields, doors, or stubborn beasts. It is heavy for its class, but tradesmen who need force over finesse understand the appeal.
flavorText: “Made for the moment when a clean thrust is not enough.”

name: spiked halberd
shortDescription: A halberd bristling with extra spikes around the head, more intimidating than elegant. It sells to gate guards and collectors of dramatic silhouettes, especially those who believe fear is part of maintenance.
flavorText: “Every added spike is another argument against coming closer.”

name: fancy long bardiche
shortDescription: A long bardiche with decorative fittings and a sweeping blade, made to look impressive beside banners or above a lord’s hall. Despite the ornament, the balance suggests it was never purely decorative.
flavorText: “Too handsome for a shed, too sharp for a wall.”

name: strong halberd
shortDescription: A reinforced halberd with a solid head and dependable haft, suited for hard service at roads, gates, and drill yards. It lacks poetry, but guards rarely ask poetry to hold a line.
flavorText: “A strong halberd is a fence that can walk.”

name: strong spear
shortDescription: A dependable strong spear with stout construction and a clean, practical finish. Its familiar form makes it easy to resell, because nearly every settlement understands the value of good reach.
flavorText: “The old design remains because it keeps working.”

name: strong spiked halberd
shortDescription: A heavy spiked halberd with reinforced joints and a head made to threaten from several angles at once. It is not graceful, but it looks like order when held by a disciplined guard.
flavorText: “A gatekeeper’s answer to questions nobody should ask twice.”

name: scarce trident of moonlight
shortDescription: A scarce trident with pale metal prongs that gleam softly in dim rooms. Night fishers, temple guards, and collectors all tell different stories about it, which is excellent for the price.
flavorText: “Moonlight is free until someone sets it in iron.”

name: fabled holy trident of electricity
shortDescription: A fabled holy trident etched with storm prayers and bright conductive channels. Priests admire the sacred markings; sailors admire the lightning tales; merchants admire the way both groups bid against each other.
flavorText: “Blessed by thunder, priced by argument.”

name: fabled arc blade
shortDescription: A fabled arc blade with a crescent sweep and edge-work that seems designed around motion. It has the theatrical curve of a legend, yet the grip shows it was meant for real hands.
flavorText: “Some blades cut straight. This one prefers a story-shaped path.”

name: rough bardiche
shortDescription: A rough bardiche with a broad, workmanlike blade and a haft that has seen more use than care. It is heavy, honest, and attractive to anyone equipping a watch on limited coin.
flavorText: “A frontier blade does not need manners to earn its keep.”

name: fabled solid gold claymore
shortDescription: A fabled claymore of impossible golden richness, more treasure than battlefield tool. Its weight and shine make practical fighters doubtful, but collectors and rulers see a declaration no banner could match.
flavorText: “Gold is soft, pride is softer, and both can still be dangerous.”

name: bloodbane
shortDescription: A named blade with dark fittings and a reputation that walks ahead of it. No merchant can prove every tale attached to Bloodbane, but the name alone makes quiet buyers lower their voices.
flavorText: “Some names arrive sharper than the steel.”

name: claymore
shortDescription: A broad claymore with a long grip and clear, traditional lines. It is too large for subtle work and too respected to need decoration, making it a steady favorite among serious arms buyers.
flavorText: “A claymore does not whisper. It announces.”

name: fabled claymore of silence
shortDescription: A fabled claymore wrapped in quiet legends, said to still the room when drawn. Whether magic or reputation, it has the kind of presence that makes bidders pause before speaking.
flavorText: “Silence is rare in a market. Rarer still around a sword.”

name: curved cutlass
shortDescription: A curved cutlass with a practical guard and a blade made for fast close work. Sailors, dock guards, and smugglers all favor its shape, though only one group usually says so honestly.
flavorText: “The curve remembers narrow decks and quicker tempers.”

name: small cutlass
shortDescription: A compact cutlass suited for cramped cabins, wagons, and alleys where longer blades become furniture. It is quick to draw, easy to carry, and popular with buyers who value practicality over ceremony.
flavorText: “Short steel for crowded trouble.”

name: large cutlass
shortDescription: A large cutlass with a bold curve and enough weight to make its intentions obvious. It sells well in port towns, especially to captains who want their crew to notice authority from across the deck.
flavorText: “The sea favors a blade with swagger.”

name: fabled cutlass of high energy
shortDescription: A fabled cutlass that seems made for motion, its polished curve flashing with restless brightness. Duelists claim it rewards quick hands, while merchants note it rewards quick auctions even more reliably.
flavorText: “Hold it still and it looks impatient.”

name: decorative rapier
shortDescription: A decorative rapier with a slender blade, ornate guard, and more courtly confidence than battlefield weight. It is ideal for collectors, formal duels, and nobles who believe danger should be tasteful.
flavorText: “A fine rapier can wound pride before touching cloth.”

name: fabled ash fiend blade
shortDescription: A fabled blade darkened like cooled ash, tied to stories of fire spirits and ruined forges. The edge carries a grim beauty that draws warlocks, collectors, and anyone fond of dangerous provenance.
flavorText: “The ash is old. The heat in the tale is not.”

name: double sword
shortDescription: A double sword with blades at both ends and a grip meant for trained, confident handling. It is difficult to master, easy to admire, and best sold to buyers who already know exactly what they are doing.
flavorText: “Twice the edge, twice the caution, twice the questions at customs.”

name: double sword with gem
shortDescription: A double sword set with a central gem that turns a difficult weapon into a showpiece. It still demands discipline, but the jewel ensures even cautious onlookers remember it after leaving the stall.
flavorText: “A gem in the middle gives both blades something to argue around.”
